clean_story: Social media’s birth year.  YouTube’s first upload. "2005" marks the moment digital life stopped being optional—like electricity in the 1920s. We remember it for hurricanes and iPods, but the real shift was invisible:—Facebook left Harvard in September, letting anyone over 13 join. —YouTube’s "Me at the zoo" (18 seconds long) went live in April. —Google Maps launched, turning paper atlases into relics overnight. Before 2005, sharing a photo meant emailing a JPEG. After?One click. No stamps, no film rolls, no waiting. Hurricane Katrina hit in August—yet the first viral fundraising used PayPal links and blog posts, not telethons. Even disasters moved at internet speed. The iPod Nano (1,000 songs in your pocket) sold out instantly. But the real nano-revolution?Cameras in phones. Nokia’s N90 let you record, edit, and upload video—no computer needed. Three related subjects are digital footprints, viral culture and smartphone wars.
